source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure API Keys Are Rotated Every 90 Days

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure API Keys Are Rotated Every 90 Days
input_row.description: API Keys should only be used for services in cases where other authentication methods are unavailable. If they are in use it is recommended to rotate API keys every 90 days.
input_row.rationale: Security risks involved in using API-Keys are listed below: • API keys are simple encrypted strings • API keys do not identify the user or the application making the API request • API keys are typically accessible to clients, making it easy to discover and steal an API key Because of these potential risks, Google recommends using the standard authentication flow instead of API Keys. However, there are limited cases where API keys are more appropriate. For example, if there is a mobile application that needs to use the Google Cloud Translation API, but doesn't otherwise need a backend server, API keys are the simplest way to authenticate to that API. Once a key is stolen, it has no expiration, meaning it may be used indefinitely unless the project owner revokes or regenerates the key. Rotating API keys will reduce the window of opportunity for an access key that is associated with a compromised or terminated account to be used. API keys should be rotated to ensure that data cannot be accessed with an old key that might have been lost, cracked, or stolen. Impact: Regenerating Key may break existing client connectivity as the client will try to connect with older API keys they have stored on devices.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the section API Keys, for every key ensure the creation date is less than 90 days. From Google Cloud CLI To list keys, use the command gcloud services api-keys list Ensure the date in createTime is within 90 days.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to APIs & Services\Credentials using https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials 2. In the section API Keys, Click the API Key Name. The API Key properties display on a new page. 3. Click REGENERATE KEY to rotate API key. 4. Click Save. 5. Repeat steps 2,3,4 for every API key that has not been rotated in the last 90 days. Note: Do not set HTTP referrers to wild-cards (* or *.[TLD] or .[TLD]/ ) allowing access to any/wide HTTP referrer(s) Do not set IP addresses and referrer to any host (0.0.0.0 or 0.0.0.0/0 or ::0) From Google Cloud CLI There is not currently a way to regenerate and API key using gcloud commands. To 'regenerate' a key you will need to create a new one, duplicate the restrictions from the key being rotated, and delete the old key. 1. List existing keys. gcloud services api-keys list 2. Note the UID and restrictions of the key to regenerate. 3. Run this command to create a new API key. <key_name> is the display name of the new key. gcloud services api-keys create --display-name="<key_name>" Note the UID of the newly created key 4. Run the update command to add required restrictions. Note - the restriction may vary for each key. Refer to this documentation for the appropriate flags. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/services/api-keys/update gcloud services api-keys update <UID of new key>  5. Delete the old key. gcloud services api-keys delete <UID of old key> References: 1. https://developers.google.com/maps/api-security-best-practices#regenerate- apikey 2. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/services/api-keys/update Additional Information: There is no option to automatically regenerate (rotate) API keys periodically.
input_row.impact: Regenerating Key may break existing client connectivity as the client will try to connect with older API keys they have stored on devices.
input_row.references: 1. https://developers.google.com/maps/api-security-best-practices#regenerate- apikey 2. https://cloud.google.com/sdk/gcloud/reference/services/api-keys/update Additional Information: There is no option to automatically regenerate (rotate) API keys periodically.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control to ensure API keys are rotated every 90 days can be fully verified through GCP APIs or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands. The audit process described explicitly involves using the Google Cloud CLI to list API keys and check their creation dates. This makes it feasible to automate the checking process to ensure API keys adhere to the specified rotation period.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_apis_services_api_key_rotation_period_enforced

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command `gcloud services api-keys list` to enumerate all API keys within a project.
- For each API key, retrieve the `createTime` attribute.
- Calculate the age of each API key from the `createTime` date.
- Pass Criteria: All listed API keys must have a `createTime` within the last 90 days.
- Fail Criteria: Any API key with a `createTime` older than 90 days indicates non-compliance.
- Additional considerations include ensuring that new keys maintain the same restrictions as replaced keys, though this is more related to the remediation process rather than the audit.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **PARTIALLY_AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is **mostly correct**—the majority of the control can be checked via GCP APIs and the `gcloud` CLI. Specifically, API key enumeration and creation timestamp checks are fully automatable.

- **GCP SDK/CLI Capabilities:**  
  The `gcloud services api-keys list` command can enumerate API keys and their metadata, including `createTime`. The same can be done via the [API Keys API](https://cloud.google.com/api-keys/docs/reference/rest/v2/projects.locations.keys/list).

- **Missing Consideration:**  
  The assessment does **not** address API keys that may exist in multiple locations (e.g., different GCP projects or organizations), nor does it consider keys that are not managed by the API Keys API (e.g., keys created in legacy systems or stored outside GCP).  
  Also, the control's intent is to ensure **rotation**, not just age—so the audit should ideally check for **deletion and replacement** of old keys, not just their age.

- **GCP Config:**  
  There is currently **no native GCP Config rule** for API key rotation, so custom automation is required.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The use of `gcloud services api-keys list` is correct for listing API keys.
  - Retrieving `createTime` and calculating age is valid.
  - Pass/fail criteria are technically sound for a basic check.

- **Limitations:**  
  - The `gcloud services api-keys list` command only lists API keys in the **current project**. The assessment should clarify that the check must be run for **all relevant projects**.
  - The program name is **somewhat descriptive** but could be clearer. It does not specify "rotation_period" or "age" explicitly.
  - The assessment does **not** mention the use of the REST API (`apikeys.googleapis.com/v2/projects.locations.keys`) which may be necessary for organizations with many projects or for automation at scale.

- **Program Name Convention:**  
  The name `gcp_apis_services_api_key_rotation_period_enforced` is **close** to convention but could be more precise (see Improvements).

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Clarify Scope:**  
  - Specify that the automation must enumerate API keys **across all projects** in scope, not just the currently active project.
  - Consider using the REST API for scalability:  
    `GET https://apikeys.googleapis.com/v2/projects/{project}/locations/global/keys`

- **Rotation vs Age:**  
  - The control is about **rotation**, not just age. If an old key is still active, it has not been rotated. The audit should check for **deletion of old keys** and **creation of new keys** as replacements.
  - Consider tracking key usage to ensure old keys are not just left unused but still active.

- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - A more descriptive name would be:  
    `gcp_apis_api_keys_rotation_period_90_days_enforced`
  - This makes it clear the check is about API keys, their rotation period, and the specific 90-day threshold.

- **Alternative GCP API Methods:**  
  - For organizations, consider using [Cloud Asset Inventory](https://cloud.google.com/asset-inventory/docs/overview) to enumerate API keys across all projects.
  - Use the [API Keys API](https://cloud.google.com/api-keys/docs/reference/rest) for more granular or large-scale automation.

- **Additional Considerations:**  
  - Document exceptions for service accounts or keys that cannot be rotated.
  - Consider alerting or reporting on keys that are about to reach the 90-day threshold.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The technical approach is fundamentally sound, but the assessment would benefit from clarifying scope, improving naming, and addressing the difference between "rotation" and "age".

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Modify** the assessment to:
  - Clarify that all projects must be checked, not just the active one.
  - Update the program name for clarity and convention.
  - Note the distinction between "key age" and "rotation" (i.e., ensure old keys are deleted/replaced, not just aged out).
  - Suggest using the REST API or Cloud Asset Inventory for large organizations.
- The control remains **Automated** but with the above improvements for completeness and accuracy.

---
